summary: Joel reflects on returning from a trip and realizing that a web design he had been approving incrementally now looked terrible. He uses Gerald Weinberg's 'sesame seed' parable to explain how incremental small changes can gradually degrade quality without anyone noticing at each step. The design had lost its elegance through rounds of tweaking, pixel-width compromises, and platform rendering differences. Joel decided to scrap the work and start over rather than try to salvage it, acknowledging that sometimes you need to go through the entire process to realize you've built the wrong thing.
key_points: Incremental approval processes can blind you to cumulative degradation — each small change seems fine, but the end result is far from the original vision
Stepping away and returning with fresh eyes is one of the most effective ways to evaluate design work honestly
Technical differences between platforms (Mac antialiasing vs. others, bitmap mockups vs. real HTML) can make an approved design look completely different in practice
It's better to start over than to salvage a fundamentally flawed result, even if significant work has been invested
money_quotes: I did the one thing that drives web design firms completely crazy: I suddenly took a look at the new web design they've done for us, which I've been approving every step of the way, and didn't like it any more, so I told them we had to start over.

Every year or two, the new management trainee looking for ways to save money proposes removing a sesame seed or two, until eventually, they're shipping hamburger buns with exactly three sesame seeds artfully arranged in a triangle, and nobody buys their hamburgers any more.
We've been removing sesame seeds from the initial design they did in hopes of making things better, and, lo and behold, at some point the design flipped from being good to being bad.
But a week later, the same basic design just looked terrible.
It's better to have something we're both proud off than to try and salvage the work done so far.
Sometimes you have to go all the way through the design process before you realize that you've built the wrong thing, but it's ok, it's a learning experience.
key_insight: Incremental design refinements can silently accumulate into overall degradation, and sometimes stepping away is the only way to see that you need to start over.
